posts.content: 2025 LLM Year in Review
posts.highlights: Karpathy posted a review of LLM developments in 2025, likely summarizing key trends and breakthroughs.
posts.worthReading: Provides a high-level perspective on the state of LLMs from a leading AI researcher.

posts.content:
LLM Knowledge Bases

Something I'm finding very useful recently: using LLMs to build personal knowledge bases for various topics of research interest. In this way, a large fraction of my recent token throughput is going less into manipulating code, and more into manipulating
posts.highlights: Karpathy describes using LLMs to build personal knowledge bases, shifting his token usage from code manipulation to knowledge manipulation.
posts.worthReading: Illustrates a practical and novel use of LLMs for personal knowledge management.

posts.content: I'm being accused of overhyping the [site everyone heard too much about today already].
posts.highlights: Karpathy responds to accusations of overhyping a popular site, likely related to AI.
posts.worthReading: Shows Karpathy's engagement with public discourse and his perspective on hype in AI.

posts.content: Vibe coding is irresponsibly building software through dice rolls, not caring what code is produced
posts.highlights: Simon Willison criticizes 'vibe coding' as building software irresponsibly without regard for code quality.
posts.worthReading: It offers a critical perspective on a trendy but potentially dangerous development approach.

posts.content: This may be the best guidance I've seen anywhere on writing a really good commit history.
posts.highlights: Simon Willison praises guidance on writing good commit history.
posts.worthReading: It highlights best practices for software development and version control.

posts.content: A short note that the predictions that LLMs would favor 'boring technology' that's once you attach them to a good coding agent harness at least
posts.highlights: Simon Willison notes that LLMs favor boring technology when attached to a good coding agent harness.
posts.worthReading: It provides insight into how LLMs interact with coding tools and technology choices.

posts.content: Visibility is the easiest piece. The hard part is analyzing and understanding what you're observing. I've spoken to teams recording 100k+
posts.highlights: Harrison Chase emphasizes that while gaining visibility into AI systems is straightforward, the real challenge lies in analyzing and understanding the observed data.
posts.worthReading: It highlights a key pain point in AI observability and debugging.

posts.content: TL;DR: More and more agents need a workspace: a computer where they can run code, install packages, and access files. Sandboxes provide this
posts.highlights: Harrison Chase argues that AI agents increasingly require a sandboxed workspace to execute code and access resources safely.
posts.worthReading: It points to an emerging infrastructure need for agent deployment.

posts.content: Resource constraints are a beautiful thing. Superior OSS models put huge pressure on...
posts.highlights: Jim Fan argues that resource constraints can be beneficial, as they force efficiency and innovation, and that open-source models create competitive pressure.
posts.worthReading: Insight into how constraints drive progress in AI development.

posts.content: The Second Pre-training Paradigm
posts.highlights: Jim Fan introduces a new paradigm for pre-training, likely discussing a shift in how models are trained.
posts.worthReading: Important for understanding evolving pre-training methodologies.

posts.content: I've been a bit quiet on X recently. The past year has been a transformational experience.

posts.content: Here is a full implementation of the Chinese Room using a printed copy of GPT-1, in case you have a few spare years and want to actually run

posts.content: So much work is going into faking continual learning and memory for AIs

posts.content: Sometimes when I demo AI, I show it turning cover letters into goofy formats (poetry, ...)

posts.content: If it helps, I teach at a business school & many of my smartest students are hired by funds because they can reliably turn their only-human ...

posts.content: EMILY M. BENDER: Yeah. And so passive, like, oops, the moon, the moon went further away. It's like no, actually, you made some decisions.
